text.title: Architect Skill Index Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that keep the architect-shaped index useful as the underlying geek/sdlc-ai tree evolves.

rule.statement: The index has FIVE lenses and only five: specification, plan-mode discipline, knowledge-base & RAG, merge-request review, governance & audit. Adding a sixth lens REQUIRES a documented rationale and an audit of whether existing lenses cover the proposed entries. Lens sprawl defeats the index.
rule.rationale: An index loses utility past 5-7 categories. The five chosen lenses cover the architect's recurring questions ("how do I write a spec / lock execution / give the agent good context / review machine-authored PRs / pass an audit"). Sprawl makes the architect skim.

rule.statement: Every index entry MUST reference a real, currently-shipped methodology by path. Stale references (path moved, methodology deleted) are REJECTED by the validate-index.py CI gate. The index does NOT author new content; it only points at existing content.
rule.rationale: Broken references erode trust faster than missing content. The architect who clicks two broken links abandons the index. Validation in CI is the cheapest defence.

rule.statement: Each lens MUST publish a numbered reading order — the architect's first read, second read, optional reads. The first read MUST be a concrete how-to, NOT an essay. Reading-order entries without numbers / shown as flat lists are REJECTED.
rule.rationale: "Read this whole folder" overwhelms. A numbered order produces a tractable Day-1 / Week-1 / Month-1 sequence. The order is the difference between adoption and bounce.

rule.statement: Each lens MUST have 3-6 entries. Below 3 is too thin to justify a lens; above 6 dilutes focus. Entries beyond 6 are moved to a "deeper reading" section per lens with a clear "read after the main 3-6" framing.
rule.rationale: Per-lens cap forces curation. Architects who see a lens with 20 entries don't read any. The cap is the cheapest signal of editorial restraint.

rule.statement: The index MUST be re-audited quarterly. Quarterly refresh detects new methodologies added to geek/sdlc-ai/ that should appear in a lens, AND retired methodologies that should disappear. Quarterly cadence is non-negotiable.

rationale.source: Maintenance of derivative documentation; index-decay rates

rule.rationale: Without explicit cadence, the index lags the tree by months. The architect sees an out-of-date index and assumes the entire tier is stale. Quarterly refresh keeps the surface honest.

section.title: The five lenses (summary)

list.item: L1 specification: how the architect drives spec-first workflows (spec-kit, plan-mode, agent-drafts-spec).
L2 plan-mode discipline: locked-execution, worktree isolation, task-fixable triage gates.
L3 knowledge-base & RAG: symbol-chunked RAG, agent-memory files, AGENTS.md context pyramid.
L4 merge-request review: graph-vs-diff reviewer, codemod refactor agent, Renovate→AI handoff, slash-command surface.
L5 governance & audit: conventional commits enforced, license compliance, SonarQube gate, signed-JWT approvals.

section.reference: The full 5-lens index with entries per lens.

example.title: Lens with 3-6 entries and reading order
example.code:
## L1: Specification
Day-1 reading order: 1. `geek/sdlc-ai/task-agent-drafts-spec-before-coding` — start here. 2. `geek/sdlc-ai/task-spec-kit-three-step` — concrete flow. 3. `geek/sdlc-ai/task-plan-mode-locked-execution` — lock execution.
Deeper reading: 4. `geek/sdlc-ai/kb-agents-md-context-pyramid`
example.note: r3 satisfied (numbered order, day-1 first). r4 satisfied (3 + 1 deeper, total 4 in scope).

example.title: Flat list without reading order
example.code:
## L1: Specification
Methodologies: - task-agent-drafts-spec-before-coding - task-spec-kit-three-step - task-plan-mode-locked-execution - kb-agents-md-context-pyramid - inc-runbook-as-markdown-tagged-steps
example.note: r3 violation (no order), r4 borderline (5 entries OK but the irrelevant inc- entry is sprawl). The architect doesn't know where to start.
